extending over left convexity

column21:
🧠 Acute Subdural Hematoma
Acute subdural hematoma is an acute extraaxial blood collection beneath the dura/at the inner dural border that classically follows bridging cortical vein injury and spreads along the convexity, falx, and tentorium.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer + STATdx synthesis. RadPrimer supplies the canonical route/backbone; STATdx supplies supplemental image/caption depth and higher-resolution selected image representatives.

🔑 Definition / Mechanism
  • Acute SDH is acute blood in or between the inner border cell layer of the dura and the arachnoid; Core frames SDH as extraaxial blood beneath the dura.
  • Classic mechanism is tearing of bridging cortical veins crossing the subdural space to drain into dural sinuses.
  • Older adults are vulnerable after minor trauma because cerebral atrophy permits greater cortical motion and bridging-vein strain.
🖼️ CT Recognition
  • Noncontrast CT is the initial screening study; use bone/soft-tissue algorithms, standard brain windows, and wider windows around 150 HU when subtle or isodense SDH is suspected.
  • Typical CT pattern is crescentic hyperdense extraaxial blood spreading over the convexity and potentially along falx, tentorium, and cranial fossa floors.
  • Acute SDH can cross sutures but does not cross dural attachments; it displaces cortical veins, sulci, and the gray-white interface inward.
  • In the 6-hour to 3-day acute window, about 60% are homogeneously hyperdense and about 40% are mixed density due to active bleeding/swirl, CSF admixture from arachnoid tear, or clot retraction.
  • Hyperacute or unclotted blood can be low density; severe anemia/coagulopathy can make acute SDH isodense.
  • Coronal and sagittal reformats are best for small falcine, tentorial, or convexity components that are hard to see on axial images alone.
🔬 MR / CTA Pivots
  • MR is more sensitive than CT but is rarely the initial trauma study; it better depicts extent, age variability, traumatic brain injury, and nontraumatic causes.
  • T1/T2 signal varies with hemorrhage age, recurrent bleeding, and CSF dilution; FLAIR is often conspicuous because SDH is usually hyperintense to CSF.
  • T2* GRE is usually hypointense unless hyperacute; DWI is nonspecific, but marked central restriction helps distinguish empyema.
  • CTA mainly shows mass effect and displaced veins; STATdx examples add spot-sign/active-extravasation teaching in a heterogeneous acute SDH.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Differentiate acute SDH from acute epidural hematoma: SDH is usually crescentic and can cross sutures; EDH is typically biconvex, fracture-associated, and suture-limited.
  • Other subdural collections include acute-on-chronic/subacute SDH, hygroma, effusion, and empyema.
  • Subdural empyema shows peripheral enhancement, FLAIR hyperintensity, and restricted diffusion.
  • Pachymeningopathies, dural tumors, chemical shift artifact, and atypical skull/dural disease can mimic or cause subdural pathology.
⚠️ Clinical / Management Pivots
  • More than 70% of acute SDH cases have other significant traumatic lesions; disproportionate shift or mass effect should raise concern for edema/excitotoxic injury.
  • Thin isolated falcotentorial acute SDH around 3 mm or less in a young patient with GCS 13-15 typically does not enlarge and may not need repeat CT.
  • Surgically relevant features include maximum hematoma thickness of 10 mm or greater, midline shift, and clinical deterioration.
  • Mortality is high; hematoma volume greater than 8-10% of intracranial volume is lethal.
  • In children, recurrent or mixed-age hemorrhage should raise concern for nonaccidental trauma.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Think: crescentic subdural blood, crosses sutures but not dural attachments, look along falx/tentorium, widen windows, inspect displacement, and search for associated traumatic injury.

isodense to underlying brain
anemic patient

column21:
🧠 Acute Subdural Hematoma
Acute subdural hematoma is an acute extraaxial blood collection beneath the dura/at the inner dural border that classically follows bridging cortical vein injury and spreads along the convexity, falx, and tentorium.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer + STATdx synthesis. RadPrimer supplies the canonical route/backbone; STATdx supplies supplemental image/caption depth and higher-resolution selected image representatives.

🔑 Definition / Mechanism
  • Acute SDH is acute blood in or between the inner border cell layer of the dura and the arachnoid; Core frames SDH as extraaxial blood beneath the dura.
  • Classic mechanism is tearing of bridging cortical veins crossing the subdural space to drain into dural sinuses.
  • Older adults are vulnerable after minor trauma because cerebral atrophy permits greater cortical motion and bridging-vein strain.
🖼️ CT Recognition
  • Noncontrast CT is the initial screening study; use bone/soft-tissue algorithms, standard brain windows, and wider windows around 150 HU when subtle or isodense SDH is suspected.
  • Typical CT pattern is crescentic hyperdense extraaxial blood spreading over the convexity and potentially along falx, tentorium, and cranial fossa floors.
  • Acute SDH can cross sutures but does not cross dural attachments; it displaces cortical veins, sulci, and the gray-white interface inward.
  • In the 6-hour to 3-day acute window, about 60% are homogeneously hyperdense and about 40% are mixed density due to active bleeding/swirl, CSF admixture from arachnoid tear, or clot retraction.
  • Hyperacute or unclotted blood can be low density; severe anemia/coagulopathy can make acute SDH isodense.
  • Coronal and sagittal reformats are best for small falcine, tentorial, or convexity components that are hard to see on axial images alone.
🔬 MR / CTA Pivots
  • MR is more sensitive than CT but is rarely the initial trauma study; it better depicts extent, age variability, traumatic brain injury, and nontraumatic causes.
  • T1/T2 signal varies with hemorrhage age, recurrent bleeding, and CSF dilution; FLAIR is often conspicuous because SDH is usually hyperintense to CSF.
  • T2* GRE is usually hypointense unless hyperacute; DWI is nonspecific, but marked central restriction helps distinguish empyema.
  • CTA mainly shows mass effect and displaced veins; STATdx examples add spot-sign/active-extravasation teaching in a heterogeneous acute SDH.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Differentiate acute SDH from acute epidural hematoma: SDH is usually crescentic and can cross sutures; EDH is typically biconvex, fracture-associated, and suture-limited.
  • Other subdural collections include acute-on-chronic/subacute SDH, hygroma, effusion, and empyema.
  • Subdural empyema shows peripheral enhancement, FLAIR hyperintensity, and restricted diffusion.
  • Pachymeningopathies, dural tumors, chemical shift artifact, and atypical skull/dural disease can mimic or cause subdural pathology.
⚠️ Clinical / Management Pivots
  • More than 70% of acute SDH cases have other significant traumatic lesions; disproportionate shift or mass effect should raise concern for edema/excitotoxic injury.
  • Thin isolated falcotentorial acute SDH around 3 mm or less in a young patient with GCS 13-15 typically does not enlarge and may not need repeat CT.
  • Surgically relevant features include maximum hematoma thickness of 10 mm or greater, midline shift, and clinical deterioration.
  • Mortality is high; hematoma volume greater than 8-10% of intracranial volume is lethal.
  • In children, recurrent or mixed-age hemorrhage should raise concern for nonaccidental trauma.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Think: crescentic subdural blood, crosses sutures but not dural attachments, look along falx/tentorium, widen windows, inspect displacement, and search for associated traumatic injury.

column21:
🧠 Acute Subdural Hematoma
Acute subdural hematoma is an acute extraaxial blood collection beneath the dura/at the inner dural border that classically follows bridging cortical vein injury and spreads along the convexity, falx, and tentorium.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer + STATdx synthesis. RadPrimer supplies the canonical route/backbone; STATdx supplies supplemental image/caption depth and higher-resolution selected image representatives.

🔑 Definition / Mechanism
  • Acute SDH is acute blood in or between the inner border cell layer of the dura and the arachnoid; Core frames SDH as extraaxial blood beneath the dura.
  • Classic mechanism is tearing of bridging cortical veins crossing the subdural space to drain into dural sinuses.
  • Older adults are vulnerable after minor trauma because cerebral atrophy permits greater cortical motion and bridging-vein strain.
🖼️ CT Recognition
  • Noncontrast CT is the initial screening study; use bone/soft-tissue algorithms, standard brain windows, and wider windows around 150 HU when subtle or isodense SDH is suspected.
  • Typical CT pattern is crescentic hyperdense extraaxial blood spreading over the convexity and potentially along falx, tentorium, and cranial fossa floors.
  • Acute SDH can cross sutures but does not cross dural attachments; it displaces cortical veins, sulci, and the gray-white interface inward.
  • In the 6-hour to 3-day acute window, about 60% are homogeneously hyperdense and about 40% are mixed density due to active bleeding/swirl, CSF admixture from arachnoid tear, or clot retraction.
  • Hyperacute or unclotted blood can be low density; severe anemia/coagulopathy can make acute SDH isodense.
  • Coronal and sagittal reformats are best for small falcine, tentorial, or convexity components that are hard to see on axial images alone.
🔬 MR / CTA Pivots
  • MR is more sensitive than CT but is rarely the initial trauma study; it better depicts extent, age variability, traumatic brain injury, and nontraumatic causes.
  • T1/T2 signal varies with hemorrhage age, recurrent bleeding, and CSF dilution; FLAIR is often conspicuous because SDH is usually hyperintense to CSF.
  • T2* GRE is usually hypointense unless hyperacute; DWI is nonspecific, but marked central restriction helps distinguish empyema.
  • CTA mainly shows mass effect and displaced veins; STATdx examples add spot-sign/active-extravasation teaching in a heterogeneous acute SDH.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Differentiate acute SDH from acute epidural hematoma: SDH is usually crescentic and can cross sutures; EDH is typically biconvex, fracture-associated, and suture-limited.

  • Subdural empyema shows peripheral enhancement, FLAIR hyperintensity, and restricted diffusion.
  • Pachymeningopathies, dural tumors, chemical shift artifact, and atypical skull/dural disease can mimic or cause subdural pathology.
⚠️ Clinical / Management Pivots
  • More than 70% of acute SDH cases have other significant traumatic lesions; disproportionate shift or mass effect should raise concern for edema/excitotoxic injury.

  • In children, recurrent or mixed-age hemorrhage should raise concern for nonaccidental trauma.

column11: Why can acute subdural hematoma cross sutures but not dural attachments?
column12: Subdural blood lies beneath the dura, in or near the inner dural border layer adjacent to the arachnoid. Because it is not external to the dura, cranial suture adhesions that confine epidural blood do not stop its spread, so the collection can extend across sutures. Fixed dural reflections/attachments such as the falx and tentorium remain boundaries; SDH may layer along those surfaces but does not cross the dural attachments. This anatomic compartment explains the board discriminator: crescentic SDH can cross sutures, while epidural hematoma is typically suture-limited.

column11: Why are older adults especially vulnerable to acute subdural hematoma after minor trauma?
column12: With cerebral atrophy/involutional change, the brain surface sits farther from the dura, enlarging the CSF spaces that bridging cortical veins must traverse. Minor trauma can therefore produce greater cortical motion and stretch across those veins as they cross the subdural space to drain into dural sinuses. When a bridging vein tears, blood accumulates in the subdural compartment and spreads over the brain surface, producing the SDH pattern.

column11: Why can hyperacute or anemic acute subdural blood be low-density or isodense on CT?
column12: Core states that acute extraaxial hemorrhage is usually hyperattenuating on CT, but blood must clot to become hyperattenuating. Hyperacute unclotted blood can therefore remain close to water attenuation, and clotted blood in severe anemia can also be relatively low attenuation/isodense because the red-cell/hemoglobin concentration is reduced. In acute SDH, this explains mixed-density low foci, swirl/active bleeding, and rare isodense cases where displacement of the gray-white interface or mass effect becomes the diagnostic clue.

column11: Why can mass effect be greater than expected for acute SDH thickness?
column12: Acute SDH commonly coexists with other traumatic brain injuries; the source states that more than 70% of cases have other significant traumatic lesions. If shift or mass effect is greater than expected for the hematoma thickness, the extraaxial blood volume alone is not the whole mechanism. Underlying cerebral edema/excitotoxic injury can swell the parenchyma, adding mass effect beyond the crescentic collection itself. This is why a thin SDH with disproportionate ventricular compression or midline shift is a danger pattern rather than a reassuring small hematoma.

column13: What is the suture-versus-dural-attachment trap, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Treating “crosses sutures” and “crosses dural attachments” as the same rule.
Safety check: Separate the skull-suture boundary from the dural-reflection boundary; ask whether the collection is constrained by sutures or by falx/tentorium/other dural attachments.
Key discriminator: Acute SDH can cross cranial sutures but does not cross dural attachments; acute epidural hematoma is typically suture-limited and may cross dural attachments.
Why the error occurs: Both are extraaxial hemorrhages, so learners often collapse two different anatomic boundaries into one memorized phrase.

column13: What subtle-density miss can occur, and what safety check prevents it?
column14:
Trap: Calling the scan normal because no obvious hyperdense crescent is present.
Safety check: Use wider CT windows, coronal/sagittal reformats, and displacement signs including inward gray-white/cortical vein displacement, white matter buckling, or unexplained mass effect.
Key discriminator: Hyperacute/unclotted blood, severe anemia, coagulopathy, or subacute timing can make subdural blood less conspicuous or isodense.
Why the error occurs: The reader over-relies on blood brightness and under-checks subtle extraaxial compartment displacement.
column21:
🧠 Acute Subdural Hematoma
Acute subdural hematoma is an acute extraaxial blood collection beneath the dura/at the inner dural border that classically follows bridging cortical vein injury and spreads along the convexity, falx, and tentorium.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer + STATdx synthesis. RadPrimer supplies the canonical route/backbone; STATdx supplies supplemental image/caption depth and higher-resolution selected image representatives.

🔑 Definition / Mechanism
  • Acute SDH is acute blood in or between the inner border cell layer of the dura and the arachnoid; Core frames SDH as extraaxial blood beneath the dura.
  • Classic mechanism is tearing of bridging cortical veins crossing the subdural space to drain into dural sinuses.
  • Older adults are vulnerable after minor trauma because cerebral atrophy permits greater cortical motion and bridging-vein strain.
🖼️ CT Recognition
  • Noncontrast CT is the initial screening study; use bone/soft-tissue algorithms, standard brain windows, and wider windows around 150 HU when subtle or isodense SDH is suspected.
  • Typical CT pattern is crescentic hyperdense extraaxial blood spreading over the convexity and potentially along falx, tentorium, and cranial fossa floors.
  • Acute SDH can cross sutures but does not cross dural attachments; it displaces cortical veins, sulci, and the gray-white interface inward.
  • In the 6-hour to 3-day acute window, about 60% are homogeneously hyperdense and about 40% are mixed density due to active bleeding/swirl, CSF admixture from arachnoid tear, or clot retraction.
  • Hyperacute or unclotted blood can be low density; severe anemia/coagulopathy can make acute SDH isodense.
  • Coronal and sagittal reformats are best for small falcine, tentorial, or convexity components that are hard to see on axial images alone.
🔬 MR / CTA Pivots
  • MR is more sensitive than CT but is rarely the initial trauma study; it better depicts extent, age variability, traumatic brain injury, and nontraumatic causes.
  • T1/T2 signal varies with hemorrhage age, recurrent bleeding, and CSF dilution; FLAIR is often conspicuous because SDH is usually hyperintense to CSF.
  • T2* GRE is usually hypointense unless hyperacute; DWI is nonspecific, but marked central restriction helps distinguish empyema.
  • CTA mainly shows mass effect and displaced veins; STATdx examples add spot-sign/active-extravasation teaching in a heterogeneous acute SDH.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Differentiate acute SDH from acute epidural hematoma: SDH is usually crescentic and can cross sutures; EDH is typically biconvex, fracture-associated, and suture-limited.
  • Other subdural collections include acute-on-chronic/subacute SDH, hygroma, effusion, and empyema.
  • Subdural empyema shows peripheral enhancement, FLAIR hyperintensity, and restricted diffusion.
  • Pachymeningopathies, dural tumors, chemical shift artifact, and atypical skull/dural disease can mimic or cause subdural pathology.
⚠️ Clinical / Management Pivots
  • More than 70% of acute SDH cases have other significant traumatic lesions; disproportionate shift or mass effect should raise concern for edema/excitotoxic injury.
  • Thin isolated falcotentorial acute SDH around 3 mm or less in a young patient with GCS 13-15 typically does not enlarge and may not need repeat CT.
  • Surgically relevant features include maximum hematoma thickness of 10 mm or greater, midline shift, and clinical deterioration.
  • Mortality is high; hematoma volume greater than 8-10% of intracranial volume is lethal.
  • In children, recurrent or mixed-age hemorrhage should raise concern for nonaccidental trauma.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Think: crescentic subdural blood, crosses sutures but not dural attachments, look along falx/tentorium, widen windows, inspect displacement, and search for associated traumatic injury.

column13: What early-stability pitfall should be checked before assuming a traumatic collection is low risk?
column14:
Trap: Assuming a small or initially thin acute SDH is stable without checking for active bleeding clues.
Safety check: Look for source-described hypodense/isodense foci of unclotted blood, heterogeneous density, CTA spot sign/active extravasation, short-interval enlargement, and worsening mass effect.
Key discriminator: Thin isolated falcotentorial SDH in a young GCS 13-15 patient behaves differently from a heterogeneous acute convexity SDH with unclotted blood or a CTA spot sign.
Why the error occurs: Size alone is used as a proxy for risk, ignoring density heterogeneity and evolution.

column15: What is the most common cause of acute subdural hematoma?
column16:
Trauma is the most common cause.
Exam pivot: In trauma, a crescentic subdural pattern should trigger a search for associated traumatic lesions, mass effect, and herniation.

column15: For acute SDH, what is the classic CT distribution and boundary rule?
column16:
Crescentic hyperdense extraaxial collection over the convexity; may extend along falx, tentorium, and fossa floors.
Can cross cranial sutures but does not cross dural attachments.
Causes inward displacement of cortical veins, sulci, and gray-white interface.
Exam pivot: this separates SDH from epidural hematoma.

column15: For isodense SDH, what timing/contexts and CT clues are source-supported?
column16:
Core classic timing: isodense SDH occurs in the subacute phase, approximately 1-3 weeks after injury.
Article-supported acute contexts: coagulopathy or severe anemia can make acute SDH isodense.
Detection clues: increased mass effect, white matter buckling, apparently thickened cortex, widened windows, and inward gray-white/cortical vein displacement.
Exam pivot: diagnose by compartment displacement and mass effect, not brightness alone.

column15: For acute SDH, what MRI signal behavior is source-supported?
column16:
T1WI: hyperacute iso- to mildly hyperintense; acute may be mildly hypointense.
T2WI: hyperacute may be mildly hyperintense; acute often hypointense.
FLAIR: often conspicuous because SDH is usually hyperintense to CSF, though acute collections can be isointense to CSF from T2-shortening.
T2* GRE: usually hypointense unless hyperacute.
DWI: nonspecific; marked central restriction helps distinguish empyema.
Exam pivot: exact age assignment is difficult because recurrent bleeding and CSF dilution alter signal.

  • Think: crescentic subdural blood, crosses sutures but not dural attachments, look along falx/tentorium, widen windows, inspect displacement, and search for associated traumatic injury.

column15: Which nontraumatic/spontaneous causes of acute SDH are explicitly listed by STATdx?
column16:
Dissection of intraparenchymal hemorrhage into subarachnoid/subdural space; aneurysm rupture; dural AVF/AVM/cavernoma; moyamoya; dural tumor invasion; severe coagulopathy; cerebral amyloid angiopathy.
Exam pivot: when trauma history is absent or atypical, actively search for vascular, coagulopathic, or dural tumor causes.
column21:
🧠 Acute Subdural Hematoma
Acute subdural hematoma is an acute extraaxial blood collection beneath the dura/at the inner dural border that classically follows bridging cortical vein injury and spreads along the convexity, falx, and tentorium.

Source Basis: Core + RadPrimer + STATdx synthesis. RadPrimer supplies the canonical route/backbone; STATdx supplies supplemental image/caption depth and higher-resolution selected image representatives.

🔑 Definition / Mechanism
  • Acute SDH is acute blood in or between the inner border cell layer of the dura and the arachnoid; Core frames SDH as extraaxial blood beneath the dura.
  • Classic mechanism is tearing of bridging cortical veins crossing the subdural space to drain into dural sinuses.
  • Older adults are vulnerable after minor trauma because cerebral atrophy permits greater cortical motion and bridging-vein strain.
🖼️ CT Recognition
  • Noncontrast CT is the initial screening study; use bone/soft-tissue algorithms, standard brain windows, and wider windows around 150 HU when subtle or isodense SDH is suspected.
  • Typical CT pattern is crescentic hyperdense extraaxial blood spreading over the convexity and potentially along falx, tentorium, and cranial fossa floors.
  • Acute SDH can cross sutures but does not cross dural attachments; it displaces cortical veins, sulci, and the gray-white interface inward.
  • In the 6-hour to 3-day acute window, about 60% are homogeneously hyperdense and about 40% are mixed density due to active bleeding/swirl, CSF admixture from arachnoid tear, or clot retraction.
  • Hyperacute or unclotted blood can be low density; severe anemia/coagulopathy can make acute SDH isodense.
  • Coronal and sagittal reformats are best for small falcine, tentorial, or convexity components that are hard to see on axial images alone.
🔬 MR / CTA Pivots
  • MR is more sensitive than CT but is rarely the initial trauma study; it better depicts extent, age variability, traumatic brain injury, and nontraumatic causes.
  • T1/T2 signal varies with hemorrhage age, recurrent bleeding, and CSF dilution; FLAIR is often conspicuous because SDH is usually hyperintense to CSF.
  • T2* GRE is usually hypointense unless hyperacute; DWI is nonspecific, but marked central restriction helps distinguish empyema.
  • CTA mainly shows mass effect and displaced veins; STATdx examples add spot-sign/active-extravasation teaching in a heterogeneous acute SDH.
⚖️ Differential Diagnosis
  • Differentiate acute SDH from acute epidural hematoma: SDH is usually crescentic and can cross sutures; EDH is typically biconvex, fracture-associated, and suture-limited.
  • Other subdural collections include acute-on-chronic/subacute SDH, hygroma, effusion, and empyema.
  • Subdural empyema shows peripheral enhancement, FLAIR hyperintensity, and restricted diffusion.
  • Pachymeningopathies, dural tumors, chemical shift artifact, and atypical skull/dural disease can mimic or cause subdural pathology.
⚠️ Clinical / Management Pivots
  • More than 70% of acute SDH cases have other significant traumatic lesions; disproportionate shift or mass effect should raise concern for edema/excitotoxic injury.
  • Thin isolated falcotentorial acute SDH around 3 mm or less in a young patient with GCS 13-15 typically does not enlarge and may not need repeat CT.
  • Surgically relevant features include maximum hematoma thickness of 10 mm or greater, midline shift, and clinical deterioration.
  • Mortality is high; hematoma volume greater than 8-10% of intracranial volume is lethal.
  • In children, recurrent or mixed-age hemorrhage should raise concern for nonaccidental trauma.
⭐ Super Summary
  • Think: crescentic subdural blood, crosses sutures but not dural attachments, look along falx/tentorium, widen windows, inspect displacement, and search for associated traumatic injury.

column15: When acute SDH is atypical or nontraumatic, why should bone windows be reviewed?
column16:
STATdx states skull metastases may result in acute SDH, so bone windows should be reviewed in atypical or nontraumatic contexts.
Exam pivot: do not treat every SDH as simple bridging-vein trauma.
